ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for deep-fried tofu; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared soy dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g (one plate of 6–8 pieces of crispy deep-fried tofu), as commonly portioned in Vietnamese restaurants and food stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tofu, fried (SR Legacy ID 16301)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for fried tofu (FDC ID 172449) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, saturated fat, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are adapted from this entry. The input record's values (energy 180 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 14 g, carbohydrate 4 g, saturated fat 2 g, sodium 320 mg, dietary fibre 1.2 g, calcium 80 mg, iron 1.2 mg, cholesterol 0 mg) are broadly consistent with USDA fried tofu data and are retained with minor rounding. Trans fat (0.08 g) and vitamin C (2.0 mg) are estimated from USDA data for fried tofu and soy-based products; monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients are set to -1 where not reliably reported for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); GI data referenced via University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese deep-fried tofu (Đậu hũ chiên giòn). GI of 15 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: tofu (plain, soy-based) with GI approximately 15 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Deep-frying adds fat but does not materially raise GI for a low-carbohydrate soy product. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 4.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 1.5 g, classified as very low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 320 mg/100 g is borderline; fish sauce dipping sauce substantially increases total sodium intake
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume deep-fried tofu without fish sauce dipping sauce or request a reduced-sodium sauce, as the tofu alone sits at 320 mg sodium per 100 g and the accompanying sauce can push a 250 g serving well above the 600 mg per-meal sodium threshold recommended for blood-pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and minimal carbohydrate (4 g/100 g) with substantial protein (10 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Deep-fried tofu is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low GI (~15), low carbohydrate content (4 g/100 g), and high protein (10 g/100 g), which together minimise postprandial glucose excursion; however, the high fat content (14 g/100 g) from deep-frying increases caloric density and should be factored into total energy management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (14 g/100 g) from deep-frying and elevated sodium (320 mg/100 g); cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g and saturated fat is relatively low at 2 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size of deep-fried tofu to no more than 100–150 g per occasion, given the high total fat from deep-frying (14 g/100 g) and borderline sodium (320 mg/100 g), and should avoid the fish sauce dipping sauce; the low saturated fat (2 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol are favourable, and tofu's isoflavone content may offer modest cardioprotective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which promote systemic inflammation; chilli in dipping sauce is a nightshade
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried tofu is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because the deep-frying process generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation; steamed or pan-seared tofu is a suitable alternative that preserves the anti-inflammatory benefits of soy protein and calcium (80 mg/100 g) without the pro-inflammatory AGE burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Tofu (Vietnamese: Đậu hũ chiên giòn, romanized: Dau hu chien gion) is a popular Vietnamese appetizer and side dish consisting of firm tofu blocks that are deep-fried in vegetable oil until the exterior becomes golden and crispy while the interior remains soft and silken. The dish is widely served in Vietnamese restaurants and food stalls across Singapore and Southeast Asia, typically accompanied by a fish sauce-based dipping sauce (nước chấm) containing lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 180 kcal, 10 g protein, 14 g total fat (2 g saturated), 4 g carbohydrate, 2 g sugar, 1.2 g dietary fibre, 320 mg sodium, 80 mg calcium, and 1.2 mg iron, with zero cholesterol. The glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without high-sugar sauces. Clinically, the dish is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions due to its low GI and high protein content; it is borderline for hypertension due to sodium content that is compounded significantly by the dipping sauce; and it is contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation during deep-frying. The dish is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, with soy as the only major allergen. It is culturally significant as a street food and restaurant staple in Vietnam and Vietnamese communities throughout Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in deep-fried tofu?
Is Dau hu chien gion suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of fried tofu?
How much protein in fried tofu?
Is deep-fried tofu good for weight loss?
Can I eat fried tofu with rheumatoid arthritis?
What is Đậu hũ chiên giòn?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried tofu (FDC ID 172449), which is an approved primary source. The values from the input record are broadly consistent with USDA data and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain tofu. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are not reliably reported for this specific preparation and are set to -1. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for Vietnamese deep-fried tofu was identified.
notes: 1. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, mineral panels, and moisture data for Vietnamese deep-fried tofu are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 2. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for fried tofu (plant-based product containing no animal-derived cholesterol). 3. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried tofu and are consistent with the input record's values; the input source (grabfood) is a forbidden source and has been replaced with the USDA approved source. 4. The dipping sauce (nước chấm) is not included in the per-100 g nutrient profile; sodium and sugar values may be substantially higher when the dish is consumed with the sauce. 5. FoodEx2 code A0EJW for 'Tofu, fried' is used as the primary ontology anchor; FoodOn URI FOODON_03315671 for 'fried tofu food product' is used as a supporting anchor. 6. Vietnamese cuisine requires Vietnamese local name only per G6; Đậu hũ chiên giòn is provided. Chinese simplified and traditional names (炸豆腐) are included as supplementary aliases given the dish's broad Chinese-community recognition in Singapore.

Added Chinese simplified and traditional names (炸豆腐) as supplementary aliases.
Populated glycemic_index (15), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (1.5), and gi_source from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for plain tofu.

Set human_condition_name to 'Type 2 Diabetes' for the type2_diabetes key.
Set monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, and all unreported micronutrients to -1 with documented justification in notes.
